title: Project Trash
subtitle: Designing the UX structure and UI architecture for a chaotic physics-based game, in a team of 14 across two cities.

intro.heading: 14 people, two cities, one UI
intro.body: Project Trash was my first game development course at Futuregames, a collaboration between our team and students in Warsaw. With 14 people across two cities, the challenge was not just designing a good UI, it was making sure everyone was building toward the same thing.

role.heading: The translator between design and development
role.body:
With multiple UX designers in the group and artists and game designers in Warsaw, I focused on two things: making sure the UI matched the overall art direction, and communicating clearly to our programmer exactly what we needed technically.

I started by researching cognitive load in fast-paced games and pulled together key principles around readability and quick scanning. I shared these with our UI lead with a clear direction: reduce mental strain, keep it fast to read, keep it simple to scan. From there I structured the components and mapped the flow so our UI programmer could build a UI tool in Unity, which he did brilliantly.

problem.heading: Balancing chaos with clarity
problem.body: The core gameplay is intentionally chaotic, and that created a real design tension. Too much visual noise and the chaos becomes frustrating instead of fun. Players need to understand what is happening at a glance, even when everything around them is falling apart.

image.alt: Gameplay UX structure showing player motivations and feature mapping

approach.heading: Designing a UI system developers could build
approach.body:
The hardest design decision was adapting our UI to fit the artists' visual direction. Our first draft did not work, it competed with the game rather than supporting it. We redesigned around a semi-transparent background with clear, high-contrast text, and that single change gave us most of what we were looking for: readability without pulling attention away from the gameplay.

I also structured reusable UI prefabs and defined the menu flow so developers could build new screens consistently without starting from scratch each time.
bullets: Clear UI flow connecting menus, gameplay and feedback overlays
Reusable UI prefabs reduced implementation time for developers
Gameplay stayed readable despite intentional chaos

image.alt: Reusable UI prefabs and menu system structure

outcome.heading: A structured gameplay and UI flow
outcome.body: The result was a UI flow that held together across menus, gameplay screens and feedback overlays. The programmer could build efficiently because the structure was defined early, and the gameplay stayed readable even at its most chaotic.

image.alt: UI flow showing gameplay screens and interaction structure

keyLearning.heading: Clarity at the start saves time later
keyLearning.body:
I would have wanted more playtesting, specifically around game mechanics and player experience. With a team of 14 across two cities that was hard to orchestrate, but it is where the most valuable feedback would have come from.

What I took away most was how much clarity at the start saves time later. Defining the cognitive load principles early and communicating them to the UI lead meant we were all solving the same problem, even when we were not in the same room.

cards.title: Research gives direction
cards.body: Starting with cognitive load principles gave the whole team a shared language for what we were trying to achieve, before anyone opened Figma.

cards.title: Design for your team
cards.body: Structuring reusable prefabs and a clear menu flow meant developers could build new screens without guesswork, and without coming back to ask questions.
